metadata.title: Why m has to be a whole number

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The wave meeting itself with no kink at m equals two, then jumping visibly at m equals 1.7, made single-valuedness click for me. I want to push the argument further. Can you (1) walk through why e to the 2 pi i m equals one forces m to be an integer, using Euler's formula step by step, (2) show me what specifically breaks in the physics if the wavefunction had two values at one point, and (3) connect this same single-valued demand to how it quantizes the nodal patterns of a vibrating ring?

props.command: The wave meeting itself with no kink at m equals two, then jumping visibly at m equals 1.7, made single-valuedness click for me. I want to push the argument further. Can you (1) walk through why e to the 2 pi i m equals one forces m to be an integer, using Euler's formula step by step, (2) show me what specifically breaks in the physics if the wavefunction had two values at one point, and (3) connect this same single-valued demand to how it quantizes the nodal patterns of a vibrating ring?
props.segment: Why m has to be a

beats.narration_text: An atom has a magnetic quantum number m. Textbooks list the allowed values: negative two, negative one, zero, one, two. Integers. All integers. Nobody says why.

card.headline:
The magnetic quantum
number is an integer —
and nobody had to say so.

beats.narration_text: The Schrodinger equation contains no rule saying m must be a whole number. It falls out of one simple demand: the wavefunction has to have a single value at every point in space. Where does that force m to be an integer?

card.headline:
The Schrodinger equation
never says m must be
an integer. Why is it?

beats.narration_text: The azimuthal part of the wavefunction is Phi of phi equals e to the i m phi. The angle phi goes around the atom from zero to 2 pi and then back to the start. Walk a full circle around the nucleus and you return to the same physical point.

A wavefunction must be single-valued. If you return to the same point in space, the wave must give the same number it gave before you left. Otherwise two answers exist at one location — that is not a function.

Demand that Phi at phi plus 2 pi equals Phi at phi. Substitute: e to the i m times phi plus 2 pi equals e to the i m phi. Factor out and you need e to the 2 pi i m to equal one. That happens only when m is a whole number.
Watch the phase wave wrapped around a ring. When m equals two, the wave completes exactly two full cycles and meets itself perfectly at the join. The endpoint matches the starting point with no kink.

Now set m to one point seven. The wave goes around the ring and arrives at a phase that does not match the starting value. There is a visible jump at the join point. Two different values at the same location. Physics rejects this.

This is not a postulate — it is a regularity condition. The same argument that enforces the nodal patterns of a vibrating drum head enforces the integer values of m. Single-valuedness on a circle is the entire constraint.
Spin-half particles are the one exception: a spinor is not an ordinary function on the sphere. It transforms under SU(2), which allows half-integer values. That is a separate argument.

A student tries m equals zero point seven. She draws the azimuthal wavefunction around the ring. At the starting point the wave re-enters with a different phase — a step discontinuity. Physics rejects this trial value. Only integers survive the round trip.
The magnetic quantum number is an integer because a wave that wraps around a ring must close on itself. Single-valuedness, not a postulate, forces m into the integers.

card.headline:
Single-valuedness
forces m to be
an integer.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. This is not a postulate — it is a regularity condition. The same argument that enforces the nodal patterns of a vibrating drum head enforces the integer values of m. Single-valuedness on a circle is the entire constraint. Spin-half particles are the one exception: a spinor is not an ordinary function on the sphere. It transforms under SU(2), which allows half-integer values. That is a separate argument.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
